(**PCI**) or **average income** measures the average income earned per person in a given area (city, region, country, etc.) in a specified year.

["American Community Survey and Puerto Rico Community Survey: 2021 Subject Definitions"](https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/note/US/INC910222). *[U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau")*. p. 90. Retrieved 2024-01-28.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Average income of an economy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Countries_by_GNI_%28nominal%29_per_capita_in_2018.png/500px-Countries_by_GNI_%28nominal%29_per_capita_in_2018.png)](/wiki/File:Countries_by_GNI_(nominal)_per_capita_in_2018.png)

[Nominal Gross national income per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GNI_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GNI (nominal) per capita") as of 2018, according to the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank")

**Per capita income** (**PCI**) or **average income** measures the average income earned per person in a given area (city, region, country, etc.) in a specified year.

In many countries, per capita income is determined using regular population surveys, such as the [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey").[[1]](#cite_note-1) This allows the calculation of per capita income for both the country as a whole and specific regions or demographic groups. However, comparing per capita income across different countries is often difficult, since methodologies, definitions and data quality can vary greatly.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Since the 1990s, the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") has conducted regular surveys among its 38 member countries using a standardized methodology and set of questions.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

Per capita income is often used to measure a sector's average income and compare the wealth of different populations. Per capita income is also often used to measure a country's [standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living"). When used to compare income levels of different countries, it is usually expressed using a commonly used international currency, such as the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") or [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar"). It is one of the three components of the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") of a country.

## Limitations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Per_capita_income&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Limitations")]

While per capita income can be useful for many economic studies, it is important to keep in mind its limitations.

* Comparisons of per capita income over time need to consider inflation. Without adjusting for [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation"), figures tend to overstate the effects of economic growth.
* International comparisons can be distorted by cost of living differences not reflected in exchange rates. Where the objective is to compare living standards between countries, adjusting for differences in [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") will more accurately reflect what people are actually able to buy with their money.
* It is a [mean](/wiki/Mean "Mean") value and does not reflect [income distribution](/wiki/Income_inequality "Income inequality"). If a country's income distribution is skewed, a small wealthy class can increase per capita income substantially while the majority of the population has no change in income. In this respect, [median income](/wiki/Median_income "Median income") is more useful when measuring of prosperity than per capita income, as it is less influenced by outliers.
* Non-monetary activity, such as barter or services provided within the family, is usually not counted. The importance of these services varies widely among economies.
* Per capita income does not consider whether income is invested in factors likely to improve the area's development, such as health, education, or infrastructure.
